text: The robotic instruments are inside the pelvis, retracting the right vas deferens and isolating the external iliac vein to expose the anatomy for lymph node dissection. The same dissection is performed on the left side. The surgeon moves to the endopelvic fascia, dissecting the puboprostatic ligaments and isolating the fat in this plane to reveal the prostate anatomy.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to fulgurate the superficial dorsal venous complex, a vein associated with the prostate. Periprostatic fat is removed to expose the prostate, with the fat sent for analysis due to potential lymph node presence. The endopelvic fascia is exposed and incised, and the puboprostatic ligament is cut using electricity to separate the prostate from surrounding pelvic muscles. Similar anatomy is addressed on the left side, with the puboprostatic ligament being brought down.

The surgeon isolates the dorsal venous complex and ligates it with a suture, controlling potential bleeding. The dissection progresses between the bladder neck and the prostate, using the catheter balloon as a visual landmark. The catheter is visualized and manipulated to delineate the space between the prostate and bladder neck, with the assistant pulling on the catheter to aid exposure.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection between the back of the prostate and the bladder, reaching the level of the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. An absorbable clip is applied to secure the vas deferens. The surgeon clips and dissects blood vessels to the prostate, continuing the posterior dissection by separating the rectum from the prostate. The deep dorsal vein complex is then incised.

The suspension suture is visible, suspending the urethra to the pubic bone. The surgeon carefully dissects the neurovascular bundle on the right side off the prostate, minimizing heat to protect the nerves. The prostatic pedicle is addressed next, with blood vessels clipped and cut, and the neurovascular bundle fully separated from the prostate on the right.

The surgeon performs urethral dissection at the apex of the prostate, carefully preserving the urethra while ensuring complete removal of prostate cancer. The prostate is freed, followed by pelvic lymph node dissection on both sides, avoiding the obturator nerve. The lymph nodes, prostate, seminal vesicles, and vas deferens are placed in a specimen bag.

The surgeon applies a hemostatic agent along the neurovascular bundles to reduce bleeding. The anastomosis is performed, reconnecting the bladder neck to the urethra. The assistant manipulates the catheter externally to guide needle placement internally. The posterior plate of the anastomosis is completed, and the suture is brought around to finish the anastomosis. The string for the specimen bag is introduced, and a drain is placed.
